paper_title: FlowSlider: Training-Free Continuous Image Editing via Fidelity-Steering Decomposition

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Continuous image editing aims to provide slider-style control of edit strength while preserving source-image fidelity and maintaining a consistent edit direction. Existing learning-based slider methods typically rely on auxiliary modules trained with synthetic or proxy supervision. This introduces additional training overhead and couples slider behavior to the training distribution, which can reduce reliability under distribution shifts in edits or domains. We propose \textit{FlowSlider}, a training-free method for continuous editing in Rectified Flow that requires no post-training. \textit{FlowSlider} decomposes FlowEdit's update into (i) a fidelity term, which acts as a source-conditioned stabilizer that preserves identity and structure, and (ii) a steering term that drives semantic transition toward the target edit. Geometric analysis and empirical measurements show that these terms are approximately orthogonal, enabling stable strength control by scaling only the steering term while keeping the fidelity term unchanged. As a result, \textit{FlowSlider} provides smooth and reliable control without post-training, improving continuous editing quality across diverse tasks.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: FlowSlider: Training-Free Continuous Image Editing via Fidelity-Steering Decomposition Taichi Endo, Guoqing Hao, and Kazuhiko Sumi Aoyama Gakuin University 6 2 0 2 2 ] . Simply specify prompt pair to continuously control the editing intensity via the scaling parameter s. Each row shows progressively stronger editing results starting from the real image. Abstract. Continuous image editing aims to provide slider-style control of edit strength while preserving source-image fidelity and maintaining consistent edit direction. Existing learning-based slider methods typically rely on auxiliary modules trained with synthetic or proxy supervision. This introduces additional training overhead and couples slider behavior to the training distribution, which can reduce reliability under distribution shifts in edits or domains. We propose FlowSlider, training-free method for continuous editing in Rectified Flow that requires no post-training. FlowSlider decomposes FlowEdits update into (i) fidelity term, which acts as source-conditioned stabilizer that preserves identity and structure, and (ii) steering term that drives semantic transition toward the target edit. Geometric analysis and empirical measurements show that these terms are approximately orthogonal, enabling stable strength control by scaling only the steering term while keeping the fidelity term unchanged. As result, FlowSlider provides smooth and reliable control without post-training, improving continuous editing quality across diverse tasks. 1 8 8 0 2 0 . 4 0 6 2 : r 2 Endo et al. Keywords: Continuous image editing fidelity-steering decomposition Training-free flow editing

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Text-guided image editing [4, 5, 7, 9, 17, 22, 31, 35, 46] has made high-quality image manipulation accessible to non-experts: given natural-language prompt, model transforms real input image to realize the intended change. In practice, however, users rarely want only one edited result. They need continuous control over edit strength, i.e., slider that smoothly modulates edit intensity  (Fig. 1)  , to support interactive exploration and to select an appropriate tradeoff between semantic change and faithfulness to the source [30,49]. practically useful slider must preserve source-image fidelity as strength increases. It must also maintain direction consistency, so that semantic changes stay coherent and aligned with the intended edit across the full strength range, rather than drifting or introducing unintended attribute changes. Prior work has explored slider-style control in both image manipulation and text-guided editing. Early approaches, particularly in Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based editing [15], achieved continuous control by learning domain-specific manipulation directions [1, 13, 16, 32, 42, 43]. More recent methods [14,30,49] learn strength control from synthetic or proxy supervision. While effective, they require additional training and couple slider behavior to the training distribution, which can reduce reliability under distribution shifts across edits or domains. Meanwhile, training-free editors based on Rectified Flow [25], such as FlowEdit [23], enable high-fidelity real-image editing without additional training or inversion [39, 47], but they typically lack principled mechanism for stable strength modulation. As shown in Fig. 2, naively scaling the FlowEdit update does not yield reliable slider: as increases, it introduces visible artifacts (e.g., ringing and over-sharpening), leading to unrealistic edited images. These observations motivate our goal: reliable slider-style continuous control in training-free setting while preserving source fidelity and edit-direction consistency. We propose FlowSlider, training-free slider mechanism for FlowEdit that enables continuous control by decomposing the editing update into fidelity term and steering term. The fidelity term acts as source-conditioned stabilizer, keeping the editing dynamics close to source-consistent regime and thereby preserving source fidelity as edit strength increases. The steering term drives the intended semantic transition toward the target edit. We control edit strength with single scalar by scaling only the steering term while keeping the fidelity term fixed. Given source image and source-target prompt pair, FlowSlider generates continuous family of edits with smooth attenuation and amplification, without post-training. This formulation offers two key advantages for continuous image editing. First, it is training-free by construction: strength control is implemented as an inference-time modification of the editing dynamics rather than learned Naive Scaling in FlowEdit FlowSlider FlowSlider 3 Original = 1 = 2 Fig. 2: Autumn Winter editing example. Naive scaling in FlowEdit [23] introduces artifacts at = 2, whereas FlowSlider maintains stable fidelity. Note that our FlowSlider is identical to FlowEdit when = 1. = = 2 strength mapping. As result, FlowSlider requires no additional data or training and does not tie slider behavior to specific training distribution, allowing the same control mechanism to be applied directly to wide range of prompt pairs and real images. Second, it is stable and requirement-aligned: the fidelity term acts as source-conditioned stabilizer that mitigates drift and artifacts, thereby preserving source fidelity, while the steering term governs semantic change and provides principled control axis for edit strength. Moreover, our geometric analysis, supported by empirical measurements, indicates that these two components are approximately orthogonal. This explains why scaling the steering term modulates edit strength while largely preserving the stabilizing effect of the fidelity term. Together, these properties yield reliable slider behavior that meets the desiderata above. We evaluate edit quality and slider behavior on our continuous editing benchmark and the general editing benchmark PIE-Bench. Results show that FlowSlider achieves the best slider behavior while maintaining superior edit quality compared with existing methods. We further validate generality on two Rectified Flow backbones, FLUX.1-dev and Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, without any posttraining.

sections.title: 2.1 Text-guided real-image editing
sections.content: Text-guided image editing [5, 7, 9, 31, 46] has progressed rapidly with large-scale text-to-image generative models [3,11,35,37,38,41,48], driven by diffusion models [10, 18, 29, 44, 45] and, more recently, flow matching [24]/Rectified Flow [25] formulations. useful way to organize this literature is by editing interface. Prompt-pair editing specifies source prompt and target prompt and edits by transporting between the corresponding conditional distributions. Promptto-Prompt [17] popularized prompt-pair control for generated images by manipulating cross-attention [38] during generation. Extending this paradigm to real images typically requires inversion or optimization [40] to preserve input identity and structure, as explored in Imagic [22], Null-text Inversion [28], DiffEdit [9], and Plug-and-Play Diffusion Features [46]. Instruction-based editing instead uses natural-language command and produces an edited image directly. representative direction trains instruction-following editors on curated or synthetic 4 Endo et al. supervision: InstructPix2Pix [5] constructs large-scale synthetic edit pairs using language model [6] and text-to-image model [38], enabling single-pass instruction editing. More recent foundation editors, including FLUX.1 Kontext [4], Z-Image-Edit [48], and Qwen-Image-Edit [35], further scale data and training pipelines, demonstrating broad editing capability across diverse instructions. These works primarily study what edits can be produced and how faithfully they can be applied to real images. In contrast, our focus is complementary: predictable, slider-style continuous control of edit strength in training-free real-image editor.

sections.title: 2.2 Learning-based continuous control and sliders
sections.content: Continuous control predates diffusion models. GANs-era editing methods [1, 13, 16, 32, 42, 43] introduced interpretable latent directions that enable smooth attribute and style traversal. In recent diffusion-based frameworks, slider-like control is typically obtained by learning explicit strength-calibration mechanisms, e.g., learning directions, learning mappings from scalar to modulation space, or training lightweight control modules. Concept Sliders [14], for example, train Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [20] parameter directions for targeted concepts and expose the scale as continuous control. Most relevant to modern instruction-driven editors, Kontinuous Kontext [30] introduces an explicit scalar strength by training lightweight projector on diffusion transformers [33] that maps strength value to modulation offsets, using synthesized supervision with strength labels [30]. They use Qwen-VL [2] and FLUX.1 Kontext [4] to generate editing triplets (source image, instruction, edited image), and then use FreeMorph [8] to create intermediate edited images. SliderEdit [49] targets multi-instruction editing by training per-instruction sliders (e.g., adapter/LoRA-style updates) to enable independent strength adjustment for each instruction. Collectively, these methods demonstrate that slider control is achievable and often effective; however, this controllability is realized through additional supervision and training to calibrate the relationship between scalar and the resulting edit effect. Our goal is different operating point: slider-style control without any posttraining, derived directly from the editing dynamics of pre-trained Rectified Flow editor.

sections.title: 2.3 Training-free editing and heuristic strength control
sections.content: A common post-hoc heuristic approach to strength control in generative editing is to vary inference-time parameters that trade off source fidelity and semantic change. For example, SDEdit [27] exposes natural control axes by changing the injected noise level and denoising duration. In text-guided editing, practitioners often adjust heuristic knobs such as classifier-free guidance (CFG) [19], guidance schedules, or the effective editing window (e.g., the start timestep), often in combination with fast ODE samplers [26]. While these controls can change the magnitude of edits, they frequently entangle edit strength with edit direction and FlowSlider 5 source fidelity, leading to non-monotonic behavior, semantic drift, or degraded preservation, particularly when extrapolating beyond normal settings. This issue is especially salient for training-free editing with Rectified Flow models. FlowEdit [23] enables inversion-free, optimization-free real-image editing by constructing an ODE that maps between sourceand target-prompt distributions. However, FlowEdit itself does not provide principled slider: naively scaling or interpolating its velocity update, or tuning CFG-like parameters, can destabilize the ODE path, amplify residual components from approximate differencing, and thereby sacrifice fidelity. FlowSlider is designed precisely to address this gap. We decompose FlowEdits editing velocity update into fidelity component and steering component, and modulate strength by scaling only the steering component while preserving the fidelity term, yielding stable and predictable continuous control.

sections.title: 3.1 Slider-style continuous image editing
sections.content: We study continuous text-guided real-image editing under prompt-pair interface. Given real source image xsrc, noise sample ϵ, and sourcetarget prompt pair (csrc, ctar), we aim to generate continuous family of edited images indexed by scalar strength R. practically useful slider should satisfy three desiderata: (i) monotonic strength control: increasing should increase edit magnitude in smooth manner; (ii) source fidelity: identity and structure of xsrc should be preserved as strength increases; and (iii) direction consistency: the semantic change should remain aligned with the intended edit across s, without drift or unintended attribute changes. Our goal is to realize this slider behavior in training-free manner, i.e., without post-training or curated data, by building on FlowEdit [23].

sections.title: 3.2 Preliminaries: Rectified Flow and FlowEdit
sections.content: Rectified Flow and classifier-free guidance Rectified Flow (RF) models learn an ordinary differential equation (ODE) that transports source distribution p0 (e.g., noise) to target distribution p1 (e.g., images). Given z1 p1 and z0 p0, RF defines linear probability path zt = (1 t)z1 + tz0, [0, 1], (1) and trains neural network to predict the velocity field Vθ(zt, t) corresponding to dzt dt = z0 z1. At inference time, sampling is performed by numerically solving the learned ODE backward from = 1 to = 0 [25]. To achieve text-to-image generation, classifier-free guidance (CFG) [19] is commonly applied. Let denote the null prompt. The guided velocity can be written as Vcfg(zt, t, c) = Vθ(zt, t, ) + ω(cid:0)Vθ(zt, t, c) Vθ(zt, t, )(cid:1), (2) 6 Endo et al. where ω 0 is the guidance scale and is text prompt. For simplicity, we write () for the guided velocity in the remainder. FlowEdit for prompt-pair editing FlowEdit [23] is an inference algorithm for prompt-pair editing that avoids ODE inversion. Given source image xsrc, noise ϵ, and prompts (csrc, ctar), FlowEdit constructs the noisy source state at any time t: zsrc = (1 t)xsrc + tϵ. (3)

sections.content: t Motivation: why naive scaling fails The difference update in Eq. (5) explains how FlowEdit preserves fidelity while following the edit direction. The term (ztar , t, ctar) provides target-directed semantic drive, while the subtracted and zsrc , t, csrc) acts as source-conditioned reference. Because ztar term (zsrc share the same noise realization ϵ through Eq. (4), their predicted velocities contain correlated noise components, and subtraction yields approximate noise cancellation. This keeps the trajectory near source-consistent regime and preserves fidelity while moving toward target semantics, consistent with FlowEdits lower transport-cost observation. natural attempt at continuous control is to introduce scalar strength and scale the update as (t). However, this naive scaling amplifies both parts of the coupled mechanism above: semantic drive and source-conditioned stabilization. Because the cancellation in Eq. (5) is approximate, scaling by > 1 also amplifies residual non-cancelled components. These residuals accumulate during integration, perturb zedit via Eq. (4), and weaken the zsrc and thus ztar ztar coupling as the trajectory leaves the source-consistent regime. Consequently, noise suppression degrades and drift increases. This often leads to unrealistic artifacts in edited images, as shown in Fig. 2, motivating mechanism that increases semantic strength while preserving source-stabilizing dynamics. This view is consistent with the motivation above: naive scaling of the full amplifies residual non-cancelled components, while separating the terms enables later strength modulation of semantic steering without changing source-regime stabilization (Fig. 3(b)). , ztar t Interpretation: semantic steering vs. fidelity stabilization This decomposition makes the role of each component explicit: one term governs promptinduced semantic movement, while the other provides source-conditioned trajectory stabilization. 8 Endo et al. Steering term Vsteer: semantic control axis. Vsteer(t) = (ztar , t, ctar) (ztar , t, csrc) is same-state, cross-prompt difference. Because both evalut ations share the same state ztar and differ only by prompt condition, this term isolates the prompt-induced directional change at that state. Scaling Vsteer therefore primarily changes edit strength along the semantic transition csrc ctar, making it natural control handle for continuous slider. Fidelity term Vfid: source-consistency stabilizer. Vfid(t) = (ztar t , t, csrc) (zsrc , t, csrc) is same-prompt, cross-state difference. It measures how the anchored target state deviates from the source-coupled reference under the same conditioning, and acts as corrective component that keeps the trajectory in source-consistent regime. Maintaining this term unscaled preserves the shared-noise coupling between zsrc , which helps limit accumulation of residual non-cancelled components and better retain identity/structure. and ztar t Decomposed strength modulation With the editing update cleanly separated, FlowSlider achieves continuous control by introducing scalar strength that scales only the steering term: (t) = Vfid(t) + Vsteer(t). This formulation yields two critical properties: (i) setting = 1 exactly recovers the original FlowEdit update, and (ii) varying modulates the semantic intensity of the edit while keeping the source-conditioned stabilizer Vfid intact. dt = Why is decomposed scaling stable? The key point is geometric: if Vfid and Vsteer are close to orthogonal, they are weakly coupled. For (t) = Vfid(t) + sVsteer(t), the update magnitude satisfies (t)2 = Vfid(t)2 + s2Vsteer(t)2 + 2s Vfid(t) Vsteer(t) cos θ(t), (8) where θ(t) is the angle between Vfid(t) and Vsteer(t). The interaction is entirely in the cross term. Thus, when θ(t) 90 (i.e., cos θ(t) 0), changing mainly increases the steering component and has limited effect on fidelity dynamics. This can be seen directly by projecting onto the fidelity direction ˆufid = Vfid/Vfid: pfid(s, t) = (t), ˆufid = Vfid(t) + Vsteer(t) cos θ(t), (9) so pfid/s = Vsteer(t) cos θ(t) 0 under near-orthogonality. In contrast, the steering projection satisfies psteer/s = Vsteer(t), so steering strength grows linearly with s. Empirically, Fig. 4 shows that θ(t) is concentrated near 90 across timesteps and strength settings, supporting this weak-coupling regime. In contrast, naive scaling applies sV = sVfid + sVsteer, so both components are amplified regardless of their geometric coupling. sections.title: 4.1 Experimental setup
sections.content: We evaluate FlowSlider using FLUX.1-dev [3] and Stable Diffusion 3 Medium [11] as backbones. For both backbones, we use =28 steps, an edit-start step of 10 Endo et al. nmax=20, and CFG scale of ω=3.5 for both source and target prompts. We evaluate continuous editing using fixed set of strength values, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, and reuse the same noise seed across different for each example to isolate the effect of strength modulation.

sections.title: 4.2 Benchmark: continuous image editing
sections.content: To evaluate slider-style continuous control in sourcetarget prompt-pair setting, we construct 250-sample benchmark from two sources: PIE-Bench [21], from which we select 31 samples suitable for continuous image editing, and set of Pixabay [34] images annotated with editing instructions following the PIE-Bench format. The tasks include season change, style change, time decay, weather change, expression change, color change, hair color, makeup, aging/deaging, facial hair, and hairstyle changes. Each sample consists of real source image, an editing instruction, and sourcetarget prompt pair (csrc, ctar). We report results averaged across all samples unless otherwise specified, and also provide category-wise qualitative examples in the appendix. In addition to this continuous-editing benchmark, we conduct generalediting evaluation on PIE-Bench [21] with the FLUX.1 backbone, following the protocol of Kontinuous Kontext [30]. Importantly, PIE-Bench is not designed for slider-style continuous control: even after excluding Add and Remove, it still contains edits such as transform dog to cat that are not naturally slider-continuous. Therefore, we use PIE-Bench only to assess generalization.

sections.title: 4.3 Baselines
sections.content: We compare FlowSlider against (i) learning-based slider methods that learn explicit strength calibration and (ii) training-free, inference-time strength controls based on post-hoc heuristic parameter tuning. Learning-based slider methods. We compare against representative learningbased continuous-control approaches, including Kontinuous Kontext [30] and SliderEdit [49], using their official inference pipelines and recommended settings. These methods realize slider behavior by learning strength-dependent modulation from synthetic or proxy supervision. Since they are instruction-based editing approaches, we use editing instructions for comparison rather than the source target prompt pairs used in our method. Training-free heuristic controls. We first compare with CFG-scaling results for FLUX-Kontext [4] under its official inference pipeline. We also evaluate two common inference-time strategies for modulating edit strength in FlowEdit [23]: (a) CFG scaling, which varies the target classifier-free guidance scale while keeping the source fixed; we use CFG ωtar {1.5, 3.5, 5.5, 7.5, 9.5}. (b) edit-window nmax tuning, which varies the edit-start step to change the effective editing path; we use nmax {20, 22, 24, 26, 28} for comparison. In addition, we include naive Original Clean Decay FlowSlider 11 Original Neutral Smiling O ] 0 3 [ ] 9 4 [ r ] 0 3 [ ] 9 4 [ Fig. 5: Qualitative slider results. We compare our method against Kontinuous Kontext [30] and SliderEdit [49]. Each row shows the original (leftmost) and edited outputs at increasing slider strengths (left to right). strength scaling of the original FlowEdit update (scaling directly) as an ablation, as it is the most direct but typically unstable way to introduce strength parameter.

sections.title: 4.4 Evaluation metrics
sections.content: We evaluate methods along two axes: edit quality (does the edit achieve the intended semantic change?) and slider behavior (does single scalar strength produce predictable, well-behaved trajectories?). All metrics are computed over fixed grid of increasing strength values and averaged across the benchmark. Full metric definitions are provided in the appendix. Edit quality. We measure prompt-aligned semantic change using CLIP-dir [5, 36], which evaluates whether the direction of image change from the source aligns with the direction induced by the source-to-target prompt change in CLIP 12 Endo et al. Table 1: Quantitative evaluation results on our continuous-editing benchmark. We compare our method with other slider approaches along two dimensions: edit quality and slider behavior. An ideal slider method should achieve strong slider behavior while maintaining high edit quality. Method Edit quality Slider behavior CLIP-dir DreamSim Mono Smooth FlowEdit (CFG) FlowEdit (nmax) FLUX-Kontext Kontinuous Kontext SliderEdit FlowSlider (SD3) FlowSlider (FLUX.1) 0.113 0.151 0.470 0.358 0. 0.436 0.400 0.128 0.266 0.269 0.179 0.337 0.137 0.090 0.214 0.530 0.324 0.588 0.391 0.739 0.833 0.61 0.61 4.02 0.85 0. 0.04 0.01 embedding space. To quantify source preservation, we report the DreamSim distance [12] between edited and source images, perceptual similarity measure designed to reflect high-level structure and identity preservation. Ideally, edited results should preserve identity and structure while aligning with the intended edit direction, reflected by lower DreamSim score and higher CLIP-dir score. Slider behavior. We evaluate whether strength modulation behaves like true slider. Monotonicity measures how consistently the edit effect increases with without reversals, capturing predictable strength ordering. Smoothness [30] measures whether intermediate strengths form coherent gradual transition, penalizing abrupt changes between neighboring values.

sections.title: 4.5 Qualitative slider results
sections.content: Fig. 5 shows qualitative slider results for time and expression changes as strength increases. Kontinuous Kontext produces minimal changes for out-of-distribution samples, as shown in the upper example. It also loses source fidelity at early strengths and shows non-smooth transitions throughout the lower example. SliderEdit either fails to preserve source-image identity (lower example) or tends to produce synthetic-looking outputs instead of photorealistic ones (upper example). In contrast, FlowSlider achieves stable continuous control while preserving source identity.

sections.title: 4.6 Quantitative evaluation
sections.content: Tab. 1 compares edit quality and slider behavior. FlowSlider achieves the best slider behavior and source fidelity simultaneously: it attains the highest monotonicity and the lowest smoothness error, while also yielding the lowest DreamSim and relatively high CLIP-dir score. Although FLUX-Kontext and SlidTable 2: General editing evaluation on PIE-Bench [21]. This evaluation is included to demonstrate general performance beyond our continuous-editing benchmark. We report both edit quality and slider-behavior metrics, and FlowSlider shows trends consistent with those on our continuous-editing benchmark. FlowSlider 13 Method Edit quality Slider behavior CLIP-dir DreamSim Mono Smooth FlowEdit (CFG) FlowEdit (nmax) Kontinuous Kontext SliderEdit FlowSlider 0.063 0.114 0.245 0.371 0. 0.045 0.161 0.188 0.290 0.093 0.350 0.593 0.545 0.433 1.64 0.60 0.27 0.30 0.672 0. erEdit obtain higher CLIP-dir, they do so with substantially worse source preservation and less stable slider trajectories. In contrast, FlowEdit heuristic parameter tuning fails to provide reliable slider control: CFG scaling is weakly monotonic, and edit-window tuning nmax improves Mono but degrades fidelity. These trends are consistent with our design: scaling only the steering term modulates semantic strength while keeping the fidelity correction intact. As general evaluation, Tab. 2 reports PIE-Bench results (excluding Add /Remove) across both edit quality and slider behavior. Although PIE-Bench is not true continuousstrength benchmark, FlowSlider still shows strong performance consistent with trends on our continuous-editing benchmark.

sections.title: 4.7 Ablation study
sections.content: Fig. 6 compares FlowSlider against two training-free alternatives for modulating edit strength within the FLUX.1-dev backbone. Naive scaling multiplies the naive = sV = sVfid + sVsteer. Thus, for > 1, entire FlowEdit update by s: it amplifies not only semantic steering but also the source-conditioned stabilizing component and residual non-cancelled components contained in . These amplified residuals can accumulate during integration, push the trajectory away from the source-consistent regime where shared-noise differencing is effective, and produce visible artifacts (e.g., ringing/over-sharpening and degraded structural coherence), rather than clean increase in edit strength. The linear interpolation baseline combines sourceand target-conditioned velocities, Vinterp(s) = (1 s)Vsrc + sVtar, where Vsrc = (zsrc , t, ctar). We extrapolate this form to > 1 to mimic stronger edits; however, it often causes appearance drift and inconsistent target realization. , t, csrc) and Vtar = (ztar The quantitative comparison is summarized in Tab. 3. Naive scaling achieves higher CLIP-dir than interpolation but severely degrades source fidelity and slider behavior, consistent with destabilized integration at larger s. Linear interpolation improves smoothness but under-edits and shows limited extrapolation ability, yielding the lowest CLIP-dir and only moderate monotonicity. In 14 Endo et al. The evaluation is conducted under the same setting as Tab. 1. compare Method Edit quality Slider behavior CLIP-dir DreamSim Mono Smooth Naive scaling Linear Interpolation 0.255 0.119 0.423 0.342 0.371 0.417 0.31 0.04 FlowSlider (Ours) 0.400 0.090 0.833 0.01 Green Snowy Calm Stormy u i S . t Original = 1 = Original = 1 = 2 Fig. 6: Ablation on strength modulation. Naive scaling of the FlowEdit update and linear velocity interpolation lead to visible artifacts and degraded structure for > 1. FlowSlider remains stable across strengths by keeping the fidelity term fixed and scaling only the steering term. contrast, FlowSlider uses the decomposed update = Vfid + sVsteer, scaling only semantic steering while keeping the source-conditioned stabilizer fixed. This yields the best overall ablation performance, with the strongest prompt alignment, highest source fidelity, and most reliable slider behavior.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced FlowSlider, training-free method for continuous image editing in Rectified Flow. By decomposing FlowEdits update into fidelity term and steering term, FlowSlider provides stable slider-style control of edit strength while preserving source identity and structure. Our geometric analysis and empirical evidence show that these components are weakly coupled in practice, explaining why scaling only the steering term yields predictable strength changes and avoids the drift often seen with naive global scaling. Across FLUX.1-dev and SD3 backbones, FlowSlider consistently achieves strong prompt alignment, high fidelity, and improved monotonic and smooth slider behavior without posttraining or curated data. FlowSlider

sections.title: A Evaluation Metric Definitions
sections.content: We provide formal definitions of all metrics used in the main paper. Let xsrc denote the source image, xedit the edited image at strength s, and (csrc, ctar) the sourcetarget prompt pair. We evaluate discrete strength values s1 < s2 < < sK. A.1 Edit quality metrics CLIP-dir (directional CLIP similarity). We measure whether the image-space editing direction aligns with the text-space prompt change [5,36]. Let fI and fT denote the ℓ2-normalized CLIP ViT-L/14 image and text encoders, respectively. We define the image-space edit direction img = fI (xedit )fI (xsrc) and the textspace direction txt = fT (ctar) fT (csrc). CLIP-dir is their cosine similarity: CLIP-dir = img, txt img txt . (10) Higher values indicate better alignment between the edit effect and the intended prompt change. DreamSim (perceptual distance). DreamSim [12] evaluates perceptual similarity using distances in learned feature space. Given two images and and feature extractor fθ, their perceptual distance is D(x, x; fθ) = 1 cos(cid:0)fθ(x), fθ(x)(cid:1). (11) Accordingly, we define the DreamSim score between the source image xsrc and the edited image xedit as DreamSim = dDS(xsrc, xedit ) = D(xsrc, xedit ; fθ). (12) Lower values indicate that the edited image remains more perceptually faithful to the source. A.2 Slider behavior metrics Monotonicity (Mono). well-behaved slider should exhibit non-decreasing edit effect and image change as increases. We therefore measure the fraction of consecutive strength pairs for which both CLIP-T (textimage similarity between xedit and ctar) and DreamSim are non-decreasing: Mono =

Following Kontinuous Kontext [30], we evaluate trajectory smoothness via the normalized triangle deficit. For three consecutive edited images Ik = xedit sk , the normalized deficit is: , Ik+2 = xedit sk+2 , Ik+1 = xedit sk+1 = d(Ik, Ik+1) + d(Ik+1, Ik+2) d(Ik, Ik+2) d(Ik, Ik+2) , (14) where denotes the LPIPS distance [50]. Smoothness is the worst-case deficit across all triplets: δsmooth = max k. (15) lower value indicates smoother transitions; = 0 corresponds to geodesic (perfectly smooth) trajectory in perceptual space.

sections.title: B Benchmark Specification
sections.content: Our continuous editing benchmark consists of 250 samples from two sources: 31 samples from PIE-Bench [21] and 219 Pixabay [34] images annotated with sourcetarget prompt pairs as well as editing instructions. Tab. 4 shows the category breakdown. Representative samples from each category are shown in Fig. 7. Table 4: Benchmark specification. The dataset contains 250 samples spanning 5 categories and 13 editing tasks. Category Editing tasks Samples Scene/weather/time season (49), weather (21), time of day (35) Style Degradation Color Portrait Attributes style change (44) time decay (38) color change (18) expression, hair color, makeup, aging, etc. Total 105 44 38 18

sections.title: C Additional Qualitative Comparisons
sections.content: We provide additional qualitative comparisons between FlowSlider, Kontinuous Kontext [30], and SliderEdit [49], supplementing qualitative comparisons of the main paper. Figs. 8 to 11 show further examples across all five editing categories. Each row shows the original image (leftmost) followed by edited results at increasing slider strengths (left to right). 20 Endo et al. Scene/weather Style Degradation Color Portrait Src: . . . cloudy sky with fog Tar: . . . clear sunny blue sky Src: portrait of tabby cat. . . Tar: pencil sketch of tabby cat. . . Src: . . . bright red mailbox. . . Tar: . . . heavily rusted mailbox. . . Src: bright green iguana. . . Tar: brown iguana. . . Src: . . . neutral expression. . . Tar: . . . happy smile. . . Src: traditional Japanese garden. . . Tar: . . . vibrant autumn foliage. . . Src: portrait of wolf. . . Tar: pen and ink sketch of wolf. . . Src: . . . shiny body, clean chrome. . . Tar: . . . rusted body, corroded. . . Src: . . . wearing white lace dress. . . Tar: . . . wearing red lace dress. . . Src: middle-aged bald man. . . Tar: young bald man. . . Fig. 7: Representative benchmark samples. Two examples from each of the five categories with abbreviated sourcetarget prompt pairs. The benchmark contains 250 samples spanning 13 editing tasks (see Tab. 4). FidelityEdit Strength Trade-off Fig. 12 plots the trade-off between edit effect (CLIP-T) and four source-preservation metrics as slider strength increases. Each point corresponds to fixed strength value, averaged across the benchmark. An ideal method occupies the upper-left region: high edit effect while maintaining strong source consistency. As shown in Fig. 12, FlowSlider consistently occupies the upper-left region across all four metrics, achieving higher edit effect (CLIP-T) while better preserving source identity and structure. Notably, even at the strongest setting (s=5), FlowSlider preserves source fidelity better than Kontinuous Kontext and SliderEdit at their weakest settings, indicating fundamentally more favorable trade-off. Furthermore, FlowSlider exhibits gentler slope: as CLIP-T increases, the degradation in preservation metrics is markedly slower compared to the baselines. This reflects the benefit of scaling only the steering term while keeping the fidelity term fixed, which limits fidelity loss per unit of semantic change. The smooth, evenly spaced progression of FlowSliders curve further confirms stable and predictable strength modulation. FlowSlider

sections.title: E Failure Cases
sections.content: We identify two failure modes of FlowSlider. Structural collapse at extreme strengths. limitation of our method appears at large editing strengths. Since the fidelity term and the steering term are not perfectly orthogonal, excessively increasing allows the scaled steering term to interfere with the fixed fidelity term, resulting in artifacts such as structural collapse and over-saturation. As illustrated in Fig. 13, for the gradual editing example (shiny compass rusted compass), the output remains plausible up to about 4, but degrades progressively for larger values, with severe artifacts at = 10. We therefore recommend using moderate range, [0, 5], in practice. We believe this behavior reflects limitation of the backbone in disentangling semantically opposite yet closely related prompts; using larger backbone may improve robustness at higher editing strengths. Early saturation for discrete concept changes. FlowSlider is designed for gradual attribute editing (e.g., aging, season, degradation) where intermediate strengths are semantically meaningful. For discrete concept changes such as rabbit cat, the target concept is already fully realized at s=1, leaving no meaningful direction for further scaling. In this regime, additional steering no longer drives semantically coherent change; instead, the excess signal manifests as color shifts and over-sharpening artifacts, as shown in Fig. 14. These two failure modes suggest that the effective range of is task-dependent: gradual attribute changes (e.g., aging, weathering) tolerate larger s, whereas discrete concept replacements saturate early. In practice, selecting an appropriate range for given editing task is important for obtaining reliable results.

sections.title: F Reverse Editing with Negative Strength
sections.content: An additional property of FlowSlider is that setting < 0 reverses the editing direction: the steering term drives semantics opposite to the csrc ctar transition. This allows, for example, aging to become de-aging or winter-to-summer to become summer-to-winter, all from the same prompt pair and without retraining. Fig. 15 demonstrates this across four diverse tasks. Negative yields semantically coherent reverse edits: hair color deepens instead of lightening (row 1), colors become more vivid instead of desaturating (row 2), the surface appears even cleaner and newer (row 3), and smile reverts to more serious expression (row 4). This bidirectional control emerges from single prompt pair, effectively providing continuous slider from to +s without any additional supervision. This reverse behavior is byproduct of the semantic structure implicitly learned by the pre-trained generative model: the steering vector defined by the prompt pair approximately aligns with meaningful semantic axis in latent space, and negating it traverses the opposite direction along this axis. However, 22 Endo et al. since the reverse direction is not explicitly specified by the prompt pair, the quality of reverse editing is task-dependent. In practice, we observe that reverse editing tends to be less stable than forward editing, particularly for prompt pairs where the implicit semantic opposite is ambiguous or poorly defined in the models latent space.
